sentences.text: Yes--
I just wanted to mention about Buchenwald.
Don't get me wrong.
Buchenwald wasn't such a good place to be.
There was crematorium.
There was a lot of people shot.
There, a lot of people were-- were beaten to death, shot to death and burned to death.
But we-- we could smell.
And we could see the smoke of the crematorium.
So the only thing why I tell you, we were special because we were fast coming from Russian prison.
And we were under the ring of communist-- of concentration camp in Buchenwald.
And they have the control of us.
They took sort of us under the wing.
We couldn't talk to others.
But we could see.
We could hear barking dogs.
We could hear screams.
We could hear shots.
We could see smoke of crematorium.
So this was going on-- terrible things in Buchenwald concentration camp.
So we were-- just real lucky that we come that fast from Russian concentration camp.
That's it.
Now, so I-- because of knowledge-- German, a lot of people decided to come to meet to all problems.
So all requests to translate.
So they start to call me leader.
But I am not volunteer.
Or I didn't work with the underground or something.
I don't want to-- you think wrong, like.
But because of my knowledge of German, I was called the leader.
So if you got request, all request and things, like translate or everything, they come to me.
But, as I said, it was a short time we were there.
And, of course, we were sent-- we were separated from men.
When-- all men were staying in Buchenwald.
And all woman were sent to Ravensbruck.
That's a different story.
It wasn't-- it was probably November, December.
I can't tell you exactly.
But I know it was winter because it was very cold when they put us on a train and from-- in the trucks into the train.
And from-- by train, we went to-- to Ravensbruck.
And Ravensbruck--
It was approximately--
It was approximately--
--approximately December--
--in the--
--or January, 1944?
It was approximately, yes, December, like December, might be where we were in Ravensbruck.
Might be like December or beginning of January-- I can't-- I can't yet--
--remember this.
But what happened-- it was the same procedure.
The only thing-- Ravensbruck is much bigger.
And not only that, it's-- it had a lot of-- it had the same crematorium and a lot of blocks-- barracks.
We used to call them 'barracks.' And of course a mixed-- a very much mixed prison was Ravensbruck.
Just-- because there was all sort of prisoners.
From Ravensbruck, it went through the same procedures.
You have to jump.
They cut the hair.
They took us to the room.
We have to jump in the same thing, like basin, with white water.
And we were-- first, we were cut all hair.
But we-- we didn't have hair.
And we jumped in the same--
Disinfectant.
--disinfectant.
We come out.
But this time, we had a uniform-- white and blue stripes-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] dress and a jacket.
Because it was winter, it was a dress and a jacket.
But in summertime, we had only dress.
Everybody could identify what kind of prisoner he is by wearing a wrinkle--
A triangle.
--a triangle.
A red triangle with the R said she's a Russian political prisoner.
Under the triangle was a white piece of material where was your number.
My number was 3-- 23955, and so on.
Everyone had-- was identified by the-- on your dress.
--on the--
Now, there was a red triangle.
There was green triangle.
There were-- green was-- how you call them?
Criminal.
Black was gypsies.
And red was-- the only stars have Jewish.
But in Ravensbruck, you couldn't see many of these yellow stars at all.
I didn't see-- but always, I didn't see many Jews at all-- Jewish in Ravensbruck.
Every one of them have a separate blocks.
Every category a separate--
Every category, yes.
The prisoners of Russia-- the prisoners of Polish have P.
A prisoner of French, F-- political.
They is-- was French who was green.
So they have another block for green, for black, and for red.
Identification where they come from was the letter.
You knew where they come.
Funny, but true-- I didn't see not on the black letters, not on the green letters.
It was just green-- green-- green triangle, but not letters.
Sorry, you didn't see--
You see, on the-- on the red, political, they put the first name of your country.
R, Russian, Pole, Polish, F-- but if you wear green, there was no letters on the green.
You were-- you were criminal.
That's all.
They was treated differently.
Political-- political were different thing.
Green was different thing.
And black hasn't-- didn't have letters, either, whether they were in Romania-- from Romania, Hungary, or this.
They just have a black-- black triangle.
Only the red have name of the country.
So of course, the first day, we start to work.
What there was not much work in the-- on the ground.
So they did-- they put the gravel on the roads-- on the-- the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] footpaths or something.
And we have to go with those big rollers and push the rollers to settle them, the gravel.
And put again-- with the shovels, the gravel, and again do it.
And it was there from 6 o'clock in the morning.
It was Appell.
We were all staying.
They was calling.
And we start to work.
This-- this group work this.
This does that.
And this does that.
And from then, I was-- from Ravensbruck, they sent there for all kind of work.
And they sent me.
Liuda, she was sent to the same factory where we used to do parts for the air forces.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we start to work down there.
And that was not far between-- it was not far from Ravensbruck.
But I-- I couldn't tell you exactly.
It's not far from Dusseldorf, I suppose.
There was the big factories.
And we were put down there, most of the prisoners.
Because the first time you come there, they put you on a worker there.
But they haven't got-- most-- if you're sick, in-- in-- in Ravensbruck-- if you're sick, they take you straight away to crematorium.
They never bother with the sick people.
If you have a physical strength and if you're young, you have a chance to survive.
We never-- never thought with terms 'life'-- prison.
We were thinking of another year, another year, another two years.
We have survived us two or three years.
And maybe, we will be free.
I got a life-- concentration camp.
But I didn't think with terms 'life.' Because I never lost faith that German can't-- can't win the war.
I was pretty sure that they will lose it.
Because they did horrible thing.
People bound to find out.
They're bound to unite against them.
And the people united against one enemy.
It's impossible to win a war.
So we live with one day at a time.
The longer we can survive, then more chances we be-- we can be free.
Were you with anyone in particular in this camp?
Pardon?
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In this camp, I start to find out whom I can trust and whom I can't trust, whom I needed, and whom I don't needed, to make sure that the people needed people have a help.
Because they knew if they seek-- or somebody will found it, so they will put away.
In-- Liuda, that's friend that-- I have letter from her-- from her.
And she--
Her father-- her father was in prison, mother, and she.
And she was-- she and her mother was in Ravensbruck.
And we were sent in the same camp-- working force in--
--this--
--factory.
I can't remember.
But I have a look.
I might find out.
She sent me a letter.
But she-- she-- only Liuda, she told me.
And I can-- I remember long enough.
But I've forgotten now.
And-- no, no, no, that's Russia.
They're not Jewish-- from Buchenwald, no Jewish at all-- all Russian.
But, you see, why I trust Liuda-- because her mother and her father all were the prisoners.
So I had a fight in there.
And she helped me a lot to put people sick in hospital that we had on the premises of these factories.
So I helped a lot.
Because as soon as they were sick and I-- somebody come to me, look, she's sick.
And if she goes, she might-- so straight away, I went to Liuda and said, Liuda, you better ring to doctor, make sure she knows the name who has to-- the number who has to go to the hospital.
And she did it.
We work together.
I had even a-- a contact with the prison-- men prisoners, which were a few kilometers from our prison working on the factories, too, that way of working.
That was a factory who produce part for the aircraft.
So I was working as a welder.
I was-- parts to the, actually, plane-- to the airplane.
And because I never weld-- and I didn't now-- they have to teach me.
So I make sure they teach me long enough.
Oh, it's when master come and look at me, I make a hole.
So he knew.
Oh, you're not ready yet.
I-- I was waiting-- they were waiting long enough-- me, to be ready to weld it properly.
We weld not only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] iron, but we weld aluminium.
All sort of things we weld.
We were-- we were working on the gas-- bicarbonate gas, [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know--
--how you call it, the cylinders.
But two cylinders we have to put this-- actually, weld that to the two cylinders, and it-- to put the fire to-- to weld.
And, of course, that gives me opportunity to get in touch with the man's concentration camp.
The cylinders was brought by the prisoners of the next men's concentration camp on the-- how you call-- trolley, you would call.
The cylinders was lying on a trolley.
And about four or five of them were bringing this trolley to the place-- special place in the corner.
And we-- if we needed, we have to tag the cylinders, put down there light-- there empty, and tag the full one to put back to be able to work.
And there was one prisoner which is-- was, like, a head of this group, used to look at me when I was [INAUDIBLE].
And one time, I look at him.
And I says, how to get in touch with them?
What's going there?
If we need to have any connection with them, what-- so I-- I showed him that I write and put in the cap of cylinder.
And he was quick.
He was so quick that he put fast-- and then I put the second letter to find out how it's going there.
And when we-- we did once a hunger-- a hunger strike.
So it was not only me-- us-- our--
--but after--
--because--
It was not-- I understand.
In Russian-- because he-- it was Russian.
I wouldn't touch anybody else.
But you straight away knew who's Russian, who's other.
You get a lot of French which was political.
And they have an F.
He was French and green.
But oh, they was persecuted.
They was terrible.
And I-- I remember one night, they brought about five or six dogs.
And what-- it was such a scream, it's unbelievable.
Because I was-- I was a special place where we-- four of us had that table where we welding.
That was there.
But the French was working in another part.
And all screams-- and six dogs and one French girl was running.
And they was running [INAUDIBLE].
What she did, I didn't actually know.
I don't know.
But they catch her.
The dog jump on her and start to scratch.
And the all-- oh, they were so organized, French, all of them on this Aufseherin and they start to take her away from the dog.
Oh, but she was all in blood.
It was shocking to look-- shocking to look.
Look, oh, a lot of people in this position did things that they-- unbelievable, even me.
I was risking with everything.
But the life-- I remember I spit to my Aufseherin in the face.
And I was OK what she will do.
I just was lucky.
She said, two prisoners to bring two buckets of water.
She put over me cold water.
I will stay from the morning till the night in the wet-- cold.
But they didn't kill me.
I'm still alive, you see.
But the people were strong and were very weak.
Because I-- a doctor, which was in my Stube, in my-- the same room with-- where I was-- I lived.
She throw herself on the wire, the high voltage.
She burned-- in two minutes, ashes left.
She couldn't take it anymore.
She couldn't.
Most of-- intelligent people who wasn't physically strong, they break.
When you're physically strong, it's not so easy mentally to break.
But when you're not strong and you're feeling down, for those people was very hard.
And they couldn't take it.
They take the lives.
And there was a few.
Yes.
Yes.
I saw--
From then on-- we elaborate.
And when we decided to do a strike against-- because we didn't have enough bread.
It was a little piece of bread in the morning, you know, at night, and soup.
And we couldn't get-- and we were working pretty hard.
It's-- it's not only-- it's why we have to pick up big things and put it in the form, and I was-- very heavy.
And of course-- so they decided, too.
And we did it together.
Did-- so through the letter.
The first--
Huh?
Oh, no.
No.
We never spoke.
I can only show him on the [POINTS] finger-- there or that or this.
And he knew whether it was in cylinder or it was-- before later on, I was very frightened of cylinder.
If somebody else will pick up the cylinder in there, tighten, they might found.
And I didn't want.
So we find a spot.
See, we have a special room where all-- all those wire-- wire needed for the welding, you know.
You-- in one hand, you were-- you were holding wire.
In another hand, you-- the wire-- all wire wasn't on the spot where we're welding.
They was in a special room.
And, of course, I and four of us that weld, we have access.
If needed, we go with no-- we just have to-- said, Master, I have-- and look, there is no.
So he even can send me.
Look, bring for them and for itself.
Or if I need it only, I go for myself and bring this wire.
So near the wire in the special room, there was a-- a bush.
So in one of the letters in there, I said, from now on, the letters will be in the bush.
And I explained where.
Because he have to bring wire, too, not only cylinder.
He used to bring the wire and put there.
Then he brought the cylinders in the hall there.
So he knew where it is.
And from then on, everything went by letter through-- whether-- sometimes, it wasn't time, so I showed him there.
[POINTS] He knew that there.
[POINTS] Or I showed him this, by-- so he knew down there.
Because we were not sort of-- could do it whenever we wanted.
We have to look out and look for time when we can do it.
So that's how we communicate.
Whenever needed, we communicated.
Or when they start-- the Russians start to come closer, and they-- they were occupied-- taken away early.
And I knew they taken further up north about a month or two before us.
We didn't know yet.
But they already have been ready and knew that they were going.
So later on, he-- when he wrote me, he said, most probably he will be, but when, I don't know.
You see?
And before that, you had organized the strike?
But-- yeah, yeah.
Oh, we did a big strike.
Pardon?
We--
So we didn't need to-- we stay outside.
We didn't want to work.
If they didn't go, we didn't go.
The whole camp there.
And they couldn't go a thing.
They couldn't--
Two camps-- men camps and-- but you know how many you have barracks?
I can't-- our barrack was 31.
So can you imagine how many barracks?
But that wasn't the last one.
So it was a lot of barracks.
It was a big camp-- big.
And it was a big factory--
--on strike.
Totally-- strike.
So we didn't go to work.
In the morning, when they start to scream, we went out.
We all went out because if we stay, I don't know.
I would've died.
But we all stand up.
And we didn't move.
And--
--we said--
Oh, we-- we stand about five hours.
But we got a little piece more of bread.
And it will come like this.
[POINTS] But what-- but what we have-- the worst thing, always weekend when we didn't work, what they decide.
Every second weekend Sunday, what they decide is to do searching.
So at 6 o'clock, we got up.
Everybody got out, went there, somewhere, whatever.
And they have to search everyone's room for any metal any things, whatever-- there.
And we use to-- they put us outside in the snow with those wooden-- is-- with-- I-- I didn't-- I could say I was barefoot in the snow for 12 hours.
And not only me-- the whole camp.
How often did--
The whole--
Every two, three weeks, for sure, they did this to us.
So instead to have a rest on Sunday-- that was the only day that we didn't work.
So they did this to us.
From the morning till the night, just like work.
Even worse, because you stay-- you can't work, move, nothing.
And we don't eat, either.
That's what they did to us every two, three weeks.
And they call it 'searching.' They search for what we put away or what we took or what we shouldn't have, or whatever.
Always they find out.
And of course, there was kapo in every one, which helped, of course.
The-- the-- nah.
You see, we worked for German purpose.
They needed this work-- be done.
I-- that's what I presume.
But they didn't-- they punish us later on.
From then on, we-- we starting to have Appells.
It's not only searching-- Appells.
You were staying-- we come from work.
You're staying till 12 o'clock night in the snow, staying, doing nothing.
And not-- wouldn't give us even-- even coffee or whatever-- that was Nazi punishment.
We couldn't do it.
Oh, for a long time-- long time, much longer than we stayed there, much longer.
The just say Appell, and we go.
And we-- the most important thing-- it was unexpected.
We never knew what's going to happen, when it's going to happen.
They just said, out!
And that's it.
And we didn't now what-- what for.
Now-- later on, we realize that this is our punishment.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because before, it wasn't.
Before, they did only search.
And it was understandable.
But when we come back, we have to do our mattresses-- put back, all the-- how do you call it-- beds making and all sort of things.
Because everything was a mess.
And we have to make it good.
Straw mattresses--
--straw mattresses--
--yeah.
There were in the-- in the-- of course, in the-- this cotton-like, dark cotton, remember.
But it was mattresses.
But our leisure time, when we come from work, when we haven't got Appell, and they doesn't-- doesn't take us out, what we do-- we take off our things.
And we were knocking the lice-- lice.
We have lice in our clothes.
And we have to clean them.
They were killing-- we couldn't wash up.
We could wash once a week.
Can you imagine-- work like a horse all day, come home, not be able to take off and put something else, but to wear the same thing?
Sometimes we did, when it was warmer, we could do it at the-- night.
But-- and put on, when it's-- heater was on.
But it was half-wet.
And we put in the morning and go to work.
So of course, we all had-- we didn't have hair.
But we have lice in the dress.
It was a big problem.
Or the-- oh, it was [INAUDIBLE].
It-- comes sickness-- we have a lot of sickness of typhus because of that.
They brought.
That's why I had this-- you know how many people survived in hospital because I sent them there?
They would have been killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knocked this whole thing.
Me and Liuda, she was sitting on her bed, I on my bed, and taking out it like this between that.
And you could hear when you kill.
If you don't kill, you do it again so that you kill them.
